walkthrough.title: Unfold the Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Clue set up. First, unfold the game board and lay it flat in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. The board shows a mansion with nine rooms connected by hallways. You will also see a small square in the center of the board for the Confidential envelope. Make sure everyone can see all the rooms clearly.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Cards into Three Decks
walkthrough.text: Find all twenty-one cards and sort them into three separate piles. There are six suspect cards, six weapon cards, and nine room cards. You can tell them apart by their backs or by the type of thing pictured on each card. Keep the three piles face down and separate for now.

walkthrough.title: Create the Confidential Envelope
walkthrough.text: Without looking, shuffle each of the three piles separately. Then draw one card from each pile, still face down, and slide them into the Confidential envelope. This envelope now holds the solution to the mystery: one suspect, one weapon, and one room. Nobody should know what is inside. Place the sealed envelope on the center of the board.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Remaining Cards
walkthrough.text: Now shuffle all the remaining cards together into one big face-down deck. That is eighteen cards total. Deal them out evenly to all players, one at a time, going clockwise. Some players may end up with one more card than others, and that is perfectly fine. Look at your own cards but keep them hidden from everyone else.

walkthrough.title: Mark Your Detective Notepad
walkthrough.text: Give each player a detective notepad sheet and a pencil. The notepad lists all six suspects, all six weapons, and all nine rooms. Immediately check off every card you are holding, because you know those items are not in the envelope. This is your starting information. You will add more deductions as the game goes on.

walkthrough.title: Place Tokens and Choose Characters
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a suspect character and places that token on its matching starting square on the board. Place the six weapon tokens, one in each of six different rooms. It does not matter which weapon goes in which room. The player who chose Miss Scarlet goes first, and play proceeds clockwise from there.

bullets: Each player picks a suspect token → place on starting square

walkthrough.title: Goal of the Game
walkthrough.text: Let's learn how Clue works. The goal is simple: figure out which suspect committed the murder, with which weapon, and in which room. Those three cards are hidden in the Confidential envelope. You will gather clues by making Suggestions and watching what other players can or cannot disprove. The first player to correctly name all three wins the game.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn: Roll and Move
walkthrough.text: On your turn, roll the dice and move your token up to that many spaces along the hallways. You can move up, down, left, or right, but not diagonally. You cannot pass through a space occupied by another player's token, and you cannot enter the same room you just left on your previous turn unless you use a secret passage. Doorways are the only way into and out of rooms.

walkthrough.title: Entering a Room
walkthrough.text: When you enter a room, you stop moving immediately, even if you have spaces left on your roll. You do not need an exact roll to enter a room. Once inside, you may make a Suggestion. You can also skip the dice roll entirely if you use a secret passage. The Kitchen connects to the Study, and the Conservatory connects to the Lounge. Just announce you are using the passage and move your token directly.

walkthrough.title: Making a Suggestion
walkthrough.text: When you are in a room, you may make a Suggestion. Name three things: the room you are currently in, any suspect, and any weapon. For example: I suggest it was Colonel Mustard, with the Candlestick, in the Kitchen. You must name the room you are in. Move the named suspect's token and the named weapon token into your room. You can suggest cards you actually hold in your hand as a bluffing tactic.

walkthrough.title: Disproving a Suggestion
walkthrough.text: After you make a Suggestion, the player to your left must check their hand for any of the three cards you named. If they have one or more matching cards, they must secretly show you exactly one of them. Only you get to see it. If that player has none of the three cards, the next player clockwise checks, and so on around the table. As soon as any player shows you a card, the disproving stops. If nobody can disprove your Suggestion, that is very valuable information.

walkthrough.title: Recording Clues
walkthrough.text: Every time you see a card during the disprove step, mark it on your detective notepad. That card is not in the envelope. Also pay close attention when someone cannot disprove a Suggestion. If a player passes because they have none of the three named cards, that tells you something important about what they do not hold. Good note-taking is the key to winning Clue.

walkthrough.title: Making an Accusation
walkthrough.text: When you are confident you know the solution, you can make an Accusation on your turn. You can do this from anywhere on the board, not just from inside a room. Name the suspect, weapon, and room you believe are in the envelope. Then secretly look inside the Confidential envelope without showing anyone else. If you are correct, you win the game! Show the cards to everyone as proof.

walkthrough.title: Wrong Accusation Penalty
walkthrough.text: If your Accusation is wrong, you are eliminated from the game. Put the cards back in the envelope without showing anyone. You can no longer move or make Suggestions, but you still participate in disproving other players' Suggestions by showing cards from your hand. You only get one Accusation per game, so do not guess until you are sure.

walkthrough.title: Suggestion vs Accusation
walkthrough.text: This is the most important distinction in Clue. A Suggestion is safe. You make it when you enter a room, and other players try to disprove it. You use Suggestions to gather information. An Accusation is a final answer. You name the suspect, weapon, and room and check the envelope. Get it right, you win. Get it wrong, you are out. Never confuse the two. Use Suggestions to narrow down, and only Accuse when you are certain.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: The game ends as soon as someone makes a correct Accusation. That player wins immediately. If all remaining players have made incorrect Accusations and been eliminated, the last player standing wins by default. Remember, patience and careful deduction beat reckless guessing every time.

summary.title: Goal of the Game
bullets: Deduce which suspect, weapon, and room are in the Confidential envelope
First correct Accusation wins

walkthrough.title: Welcome & Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Clue! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through several turns with five players: Miss Scarlet, Colonel Mustard, Mrs. White, Mr. Green, and Mrs. Peacock. We will follow their moves, Suggestions, and deductions so you can see how the game flows. Follow along even if you have a different number of players at your table.

walkthrough.title: Setup Recap
walkthrough.text: The board is set up with one suspect card, one weapon card, and one room card sealed in the Confidential envelope. The remaining eighteen cards have been dealt out. With five players, three players have four cards and two players have three cards. Everyone has checked off their own cards on their detective notepads. Miss Scarlet goes first.

walkthrough.title: Miss Scarlet's First Turn
walkthrough.text: Miss Scarlet rolls a six and moves into the Lounge. She makes a Suggestion: I suggest it was Professor Plum, with the Rope, in the Lounge. Remember, she must name the room she is in. Colonel Mustard, sitting to her left, checks his hand. He has the Rope card, so he secretly shows it to Miss Scarlet. She marks Rope on her notepad as not in the envelope. Great start!

walkthrough.title: Colonel Mustard's Turn
walkthrough.text: Colonel Mustard rolls a four and moves into the Dining Room. He suggests Colonel Mustard, with the Knife, in the Dining Room. Yes, you can suggest yourself as the suspect! Mrs. White, to his left, checks her hand. She does not have any of those three cards, so she passes. Mr. Green checks next and shows Colonel Mustard one card secretly. Mustard marks it on his notepad.

walkthrough.title: Tracking Who Passed
walkthrough.text: Did you catch that? Mrs. White could not disprove Mustard's Suggestion. That means she does not hold the Colonel Mustard card, the Knife card, or the Dining Room card. Every player at the table should note this on their notepad. Tracking who cannot disprove is just as important as tracking what cards you are shown directly.

walkthrough.title: Mrs. White's Turn
walkthrough.text: Mrs. White rolls a five and enters the Kitchen. She suggests Mrs. Peacock, with the Candlestick, in the Kitchen. Mr. Green checks and passes. Mrs. Peacock checks and shows Mrs. White one card. Miss Scarlet does not need to check because the Suggestion was already disproved. Mrs. White marks the shown card on her notepad.

walkthrough.title: Mr. Green's Turn
walkthrough.text: Mr. Green rolls a three and moves through the hallway but does not reach a room. Since he is not in a room, he cannot make a Suggestion this turn. His turn ends after moving. Sometimes the dice do not cooperate, and that is okay. Getting into rooms is important, but you will have plenty of turns.

walkthrough.title: Mrs. Peacock's Turn
walkthrough.text: Mrs. Peacock uses the secret passage from the Conservatory to the Lounge. No dice roll needed! She suggests Mr. Green, with the Lead Pipe, in the Lounge. Going around clockwise from Mrs. Peacock, Miss Scarlet checks and passes. Colonel Mustard checks and passes. Mrs. White checks and passes. Mr. Green finally shows Mrs. Peacock one card. Three players could not disprove. That is extremely valuable information for Mrs. Peacock.

walkthrough.title: The Deduction Process
walkthrough.text: Let's pause and think like Mrs. Peacock. Three players passed before Mr. Green showed a card. That means Miss Scarlet, Colonel Mustard, and Mrs. White all lack the Mr. Green card, the Lead Pipe card, and the Lounge card. Mrs. Peacock should carefully note all of this. Combined with her own hand and previous clues, she might be able to narrow down the solution significantly from just this one Suggestion.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Premature Accusation
walkthrough.text: Back to Miss Scarlet's second turn. She rolls and enters the Study. She has narrowed down the weapon and the room, but she is not completely sure about the suspect. She thinks it is probably Professor Plum. Feeling confident, she decides to make an Accusation: Professor Plum, with the Wrench, in the Ballroom. She checks the envelope and it is wrong! The suspect was actually Mrs. Peacock. Miss Scarlet is eliminated.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Never Guess on an Accusation
walkthrough.text: Miss Scarlet made a classic beginner mistake. She was not one hundred percent sure about the suspect but guessed anyway. In Clue, you only get one Accusation for the entire game. If you are wrong, you are out. She should have made another Suggestion to test Professor Plum first. Always use Suggestions to confirm your theory before making an Accusation. Probably right is not good enough.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Ignoring Negative Information
walkthrough.text: Colonel Mustard has been keeping track of the cards shown to him, but he has not been writing down when other players pass during Suggestions. Mrs. White passed on his earlier Suggestion, which told him she lacks three specific cards. But he forgot to note it. Now he is missing key deductions that could help him narrow things down faster. He is playing with incomplete information.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Track Everything
walkthrough.text: Negative information is just as powerful as positive information in Clue. When a player cannot disprove a Suggestion, you know they do not hold any of those three cards. Write it down every single time. The best Clue players track not just what cards they have seen, but which players have passed on which Suggestions. This lets you cross-reference and deduce cards nobody has shown you directly.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Staying in the Same Room
walkthrough.text: Mrs. White is in the Kitchen and she likes it there. On her next turn, she decides to stay in the Kitchen and make another Suggestion naming the Kitchen. The problem is she already tested this room last turn. She is getting confirmations about cards she might already know, instead of visiting a new room to test new combinations. She is wasting turns by not exploring.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Move to New Rooms
walkthrough.text: Every turn you spend in a room you have already tested is a turn you are not gathering new information. Try to visit as many different rooms as possible, especially early in the game. Use secret passages to cover more ground. The Kitchen connects to the Study and the Conservatory connects to the Lounge. Planning your route through the mansion helps you test more rooms and solve the mystery faster.

walkthrough.title: Smart Play: Mr. Green Connects the Dots
walkthrough.text: Mr. Green has been taking careful notes all game. He tracked every card shown to him and every time a player passed. He now knows the weapon is the Wrench because he has seen or eliminated all other weapons. He knows the room is the Ballroom because he holds several room cards and has seen the rest. He just needs to confirm the suspect. He enters the Library and makes a Suggestion naming his top suspect to test the theory.

walkthrough.title: Mr. Green Makes His Accusation
walkthrough.text: Nobody could disprove Mr. Green's last Suggestion. Combined with everything he has tracked, he is now certain of all three cards. On his next turn, he makes an Accusation: Mrs. Peacock, with the Wrench, in the Ballroom. He checks the envelope and he is correct! Mr. Green wins the game. His careful note-taking and patience paid off while Miss Scarlet's rushed guess got her eliminated.

walkthrough.title: Before You Investigate
walkthrough.text:
Congratulations, you have walked through a full game of Clue! You saw how Suggestions gather clues, how negative information matters, and why patience beats guessing. Remember: track everything on your notepad, visit new rooms to test new theories, and never Accuse until you are certain.

Check out General Tips for more strategy advice. Reference the Appendix for quick rules lookups. And ask your personal GM Guide anytime by tapping the Q and A tab. Have fun solving the mystery!

Visit new rooms to test new theories

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the biggest beginner traps in Clue. First, making an Accusation when you are only mostly sure. One wrong guess and you are out of the game. Second, forgetting to track when players pass during the disprove step. That negative information is critical for deductions. Third, staying in the same room turn after turn instead of exploring new rooms. And fourth, only paying attention on your own turn. You should be taking notes during every single Suggestion, not just your own.

walkthrough.title: Master Your Detective Notepad
walkthrough.text: Your notepad is your most powerful tool. Develop a system beyond simple checkmarks. Use one symbol for cards you hold, another for cards shown to you, and another for cards you have deduced through elimination. Some players create a grid with player names across the top and card names down the side. When a player passes on a Suggestion, mark all three cards as not held by that player. Cross-referencing these marks is how you solve the mystery without seeing every card.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Route
walkthrough.text: Moving efficiently through the mansion is more important than most new players realize. Aim to visit a new room every turn if possible. The secret passages are incredibly valuable because they let you cross the board instantly without rolling dice. Kitchen to Study and Conservatory to Lounge. Try to plan two or three turns ahead so you know which rooms you want to test next. Corner rooms with secret passages are the best starting targets because they give you two ways out.

walkthrough.title: Craft Strategic Suggestions
walkthrough.text: Do not make random Suggestions. Every Suggestion should be designed to confirm or eliminate specific cards. Include one card you already know the answer to along with two you are uncertain about. If nobody can disprove a Suggestion where you know one card is in someone's hand, something went wrong with your notes. If you are testing a suspect, pair them with a weapon and room you have already identified so the disprove must be about the suspect.

walkthrough.title: Watch Other Players Carefully
walkthrough.text: Pay close attention to every Suggestion at the table, not just your own. When Player A makes a Suggestion and Player B passes but Player C shows a card, you learn that Player B does not hold any of those three cards. You also know Player C holds at least one of them. Over many rounds, this information accumulates. The best Clue players solve the mystery partly from their own Suggestions and partly from watching everyone else's interactions.

walkthrough.title: Bluffing and Misdirection
walkthrough.text: You are allowed to suggest cards you hold in your own hand. This is a powerful bluffing tactic. If you suggest a card you are holding, the disprove step will reveal information about the other two cards in your Suggestion. It also misleads opponents into thinking you do not hold that card. Be careful though. Experienced players will notice if you keep suggesting the same cards and never narrow your focus. Mix up your Suggestions to keep opponents guessing.

walkthrough.title: When to Accuse
walkthrough.text: Only make an Accusation when you have positively identified all three envelope cards through your deductions. Being ninety percent sure is not enough because a wrong Accusation ends your game. You should be able to explain exactly how you know each card is in the envelope before you Accuse. If there is any doubt about even one card, make another Suggestion to confirm it. One more turn of patience is always better than elimination.

walkthrough.title: Pace Yourself
walkthrough.text: Clue rewards patience over speed. Gathering one solid piece of information per turn adds up quickly over the course of the game. Do not panic if another player seems to be ahead. They might be bluffing, or they might make a premature Accusation and eliminate themselves. Focus on your own deduction process, take good notes, and let the information come to you. The most methodical player usually wins.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't Accuse when only 'mostly sure' — wrong = eliminated
Track when players pass (negative info is critical)
Don't stay in the same room repeatedly — explore new rooms
Pay attention during ALL Suggestions, not just your own

If testing a suspect, pair with known weapon + known room
Every Suggestion should confirm or eliminate specific cards

Player disproves = holds at least 1 of the 3 named cards
Best players solve partly from others' Suggestions

Misleads opponents about what you hold
Mix up Suggestions to avoid tipping off experienced players
Bluffing is powerful but don't overdo it

90% sure is NOT enough — wrong = eliminated
You should be able to explain HOW you know each card

summary.title: Pace Yourself
bullets: Patience > speed in Clue
1 solid clue per turn adds up fast
Don't panic if someone seems ahead — they might be bluffing
Methodical players usually win

walkthrough.title: The Elimination Grid
walkthrough.text: Advanced Clue players use a full elimination grid instead of a simple checklist. Create a grid with all twenty-one cards on one axis and all player names on the other. For each Suggestion, mark which players passed and who disproved. Over time, you can deduce exactly which cards each player holds even if they never showed them to you. When you figure out all of a player's cards, every unmarked card in their column is confirmed as not theirs.

walkthrough.title: Card Counting
walkthrough.text: You know exactly how many cards each player holds based on how many were dealt. If you can identify all of one player's cards through Suggestions and deductions, then every remaining unknown card must be held by someone else or be in the envelope. For example, if a player has three cards and you have identified all three, you can eliminate them from every future Suggestion mentally. This dramatically speeds up your deductions for the remaining unknowns.

walkthrough.title: Controlled Suggestions
walkthrough.text: A controlled Suggestion is one where you already know the answer to two of the three elements. If you know the weapon and room in the envelope, suggest an unknown suspect with those two known cards. If nobody disproves, you have found the suspect. If someone disproves, they must be showing you the suspect card since you know the other two are in the envelope. This technique lets you isolate exactly one unknown per Suggestion.

walkthrough.title: Reading Opponents' Movement
walkthrough.text: Watch where other players are moving. If someone keeps heading toward a specific room, they might be close to solving and want to make their Accusation from that room for dramatic effect. Or they might be trying to reach a room to test it in a Suggestion. If a player suddenly changes course or uses a secret passage to rush across the board, they may have just learned something important. Use this body language to gauge how close opponents are to solving.

walkthrough.title: Strategic Showing
walkthrough.text: When you must disprove a Suggestion, you choose which card to show if you hold more than one match. Always show the card that gives away the least new information. If you have already shown a player the Candlestick on a previous turn, show it again rather than revealing a new card. This minimizes the information your opponents gain from your hand. Keep a mental note of what you have shown to whom.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Play
walkthrough.text: If you sense another player is close to solving, you can slow them down. Suggest cards you think they need to test, forcing them to waste a turn getting new information instead of confirming their theory. Move the suspect tokens around the board to put distance between opponents and the rooms they want to reach. You cannot stop someone from winning, but you can buy yourself an extra turn or two to catch up.

walkthrough.title: The Secret Passage Advantage
walkthrough.text: Secret passages are not just convenient shortcuts. They are strategic weapons. By alternating between Kitchen and Study, or between Conservatory and Lounge, you can make a Suggestion every single turn without ever rolling the dice. This guarantees you gather information at maximum speed. Early in the game, position yourself in a corner room with a secret passage and alternate back and forth while other players waste turns stuck in hallways.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Timing
walkthrough.text: The most critical moment in Clue is deciding when to stop gathering information and make your Accusation. If you wait too long, another player might solve it first. If you rush, you risk elimination. The key is to count how many unknowns remain. When you have confirmed two of the three envelope cards with certainty and have strong evidence for the third, it is usually time to Accuse. Also watch for opponents who suddenly stop making Suggestions. They may be about to Accuse on their next turn, so you might need to act now.

summary.title: The Elimination Grid
bullets: Full grid: 21 cards × all players
Mark passes and disproves for every Suggestion
Deduce cards players hold even without being shown
Knowing all of a player's cards eliminates them from unknowns

summary.title: Card Counting
bullets: Know exactly how many cards each player holds
Identify all of one player's cards → eliminate them from all unknowns
Remaining unknowns narrow down faster
Dramatically accelerates deduction for endgame

summary.title: Controlled Suggestions
bullets: Suggest 2 known envelope cards + 1 unknown
No disprove → the unknown is in the envelope
Disprove → they must be showing the unknown card
Isolates exactly 1 variable per Suggestion

summary.title: Reading Opponents' Movement
bullets: Watch where players move — heading to a room may mean they need to test it
Sudden course changes = they learned something important
Gauge how close opponents are to solving
Adjust urgency based on opponent behavior

summary.title: Strategic Showing
bullets: When disproving, choose which card to show (if you hold 2+)
Show the card that reveals the least new info
Re-show the same card to the same player when possible
Track what you've shown to whom

summary.title: Defensive Play
bullets: Suggest cards an opponent needs to test → waste their turn
Move suspect tokens to inconvenient rooms
Can't stop a winner, but can buy yourself 1-2 extra turns
Balance defense vs. advancing your own deductions

summary.title: The Secret Passage Advantage
bullets: Alternate between passage-connected rooms for guaranteed Suggestions
Kitchen↔Study or Conservatory↔Lounge every turn
No dice roll needed → max info-gathering speed
Position in corner rooms early to exploit passages

summary.title: Endgame Timing
bullets: 2 confirmed envelope cards + strong evidence for 3rd → time to Accuse
Waiting too long risks another player winning first
Watch for opponents who stop making Suggestions — they may Accuse next
Balance certainty vs. urgency in the endgame

entries.definition: One of the six suspects. Miss Scarlet always goes first in the standard game. Her token is red and starts near the Lounge.

Colonel Mustard (yellow), Mrs. White (white), Mr. Green (green), Mrs. Peacock (blue), and Professor Plum (purple). Each has a matching colored token placed on a designated starting square on the board.
One of the six weapons. A metal candle holder used as a blunt instrument.
One of the six weapons. A bladed weapon found in the mansion.
One of the six weapons. A heavy metal pipe used as a blunt instrument.
One of the six weapons. A handgun found in the mansion.
One of the six weapons. A length of rope used for strangulation.
One of the six weapons. A heavy metal tool used as a blunt instrument.
One of the nine rooms. Located in a corner of the mansion. Has a secret passage connecting directly to the Study.
One of the nine rooms. A large room along one edge of the mansion with multiple doorways.
One of the nine rooms. Located in a corner of the mansion. Has a secret passage connecting directly to the Lounge.
One of the nine rooms. Located along the side of the mansion between the Library and Dining Room.
One of the nine rooms. Located along the side of the mansion near the Study and Conservatory.
One of the nine rooms. Located in a corner of the mansion. Has a secret passage connecting directly to the Kitchen.
One of the nine rooms. A central room along one edge of the mansion.
One of the nine rooms. Located in a corner of the mansion. Has a secret passage connecting directly to the Conservatory.
One of the nine rooms. Located along the side of the mansion between the Lounge and Kitchen.

Diagonal shortcuts connecting two corner rooms. Kitchen connects to Study, and Conservatory connects to Lounge. Using a secret passage counts as your entire move for the turn — no dice roll needed. You may then make a Suggestion in the room you entered.

A safe information-gathering action. You must be in a room and must name that room, plus any suspect and any weapon. The named suspect and weapon tokens are moved into your room. Players clockwise from you attempt to disprove by showing one matching card. Suggestions carry no penalty and can be made every turn you are in a room.

A final answer declaring who did it, with what weapon, and in which room. You secretly check the Confidential envelope. Correct means you win. Incorrect means you are eliminated but still must disprove others' Suggestions. Only one Accusation per player per game. Can be made from anywhere on the board.

The sealed envelope placed in the center of the board during setup. Contains exactly one suspect card, one weapon card, and one room card — the solution to the mystery. Only opened when a player makes an Accusation.

When a Suggestion is made, players clockwise from the suggester check their hands for matching cards. The first player who holds at least one matching card must secretly show exactly one to the suggester. If a player holds multiple matches, they choose which one to show. Players who hold no matching cards pass.

A paper checklist given to each player listing all six suspects, six weapons, and nine rooms. Used to track which cards you hold, which cards have been shown to you, and which cards you have deduced through elimination. Good note-taking is essential to winning Clue.

Roll the dice and move up to that many spaces along hallways. Move horizontally or vertically only, no diagonal movement. Cannot pass through spaces occupied by other tokens. Cannot re-enter the room you left on your previous turn unless using a secret passage. Stop immediately upon entering a room. No exact roll required to enter a room.

A player who made an incorrect Accusation. They can no longer move, make Suggestions, or make another Accusation. However, they must still show cards from their hand to disprove other players' Suggestions. Their token remains on the board but does not block movement.
